preFix.title: Polymer clay miniature fairy garden house
preFix.subtitle: A slab-built house with a tiled conical roof and carved window and door details, cured in stages
preFix.excerpt: A miniature fairy garden house approximately 80 mm tall, built from polymer clay slabs around a temporary cardboard former. The roof is a separate conical form tiled with overlapping petal-shaped slabs. Windows and a door are cut from the walls before curing. Vine and flower decorations are added as coils and balls. Cured in two stages in a domestic oven.

content.text: This miniature house is built entirely from polymer clay using a
slab
construction method. A cardboard box acts as a
former
for the walls during assembly and is removed before the first curing session. The conical roof is a separate piece built on a paper cone former.
Staged curing
hardens the walls and roof separately before decorative details are applied and cured in a final oven session. No kiln is required: polymer clay hardens in a domestic oven at 110 to 130 degrees C.

tools.name: Wooden rolling pin or acrylic roller

materials.name: Polymer clay, stone grey or cream
Polymer clay, roof tile colour (terracotta, green, or blue)
Polymer clay, accent colours for door, windows, flowers

content.text: Preparation

Cut a cardboard box down to a cube or rectangular block about 50 mm wide by 50 mm deep by 45 mm tall. This is the wall former. Wrap the outside of the box lightly in a sheet of baking parchment so the clay does not adhere to the card. Roll a paper cone from a circle of card: the cone base should be about 60 mm in diameter and the height about 50 mm. This is the roof former. Warm all your clay by kneading each colour separately before rolling. Pre-heat the oven before each curing session.
Construction
Stage 1: walls

Roll the wall clay to a slab 3 mm thick. Cut four rectangles sized to wrap around the cardboard former: two at 50 mm wide by 45 mm tall. And two at 50 mm wide by 45 mm tall for front and back. Adjust sizes to your own former dimensions.
Press each wall panel against the outside face of the parchment-wrapped former. Smooth the surfaces gently with a wooden rib to remove fingerprints. The four panels should meet at the corners.

Join the corner edges by pressing the clay of adjacent panels firmly together with a fingertip or the wooden rib. Then smooth the seam from inside the box edge. The joins do not need to be invisible at this stage, as texture will be added later.

Cut the door opening on the front wall: about 18 mm wide by 28 mm tall with an arched top. Use the fettling knife to cut through the clay cleanly. Remove the cut-out piece and keep it to shape into a separate door panel.

Cut two window openings on the side or front walls: about 14 mm wide by 12 mm tall. Positioned at mid-wall height. Frame each window opening by pressing a thin strip of clay around the perimeter of the opening.
Cut the base panel: a square the same footprint as the former. Press the wall assembly down onto the base panel and smooth the joint between the wall bottoms and the base all the way around.

Carefully slide out the cardboard former and paper lining. The polymer clay walls will hold their own shape. If any wall bows inward. Press a small crumpled piece of baking parchment inside temporarily while the structure is transferred to the oven.
Place the house shell on a ceramic tile and
cure
at 110 to 130 degrees C for 30 minutes. Allow to cool completely before handling.
Stage 2: roof
Roll the roof-colour clay to a slab 2 mm thick. Cut petal-shaped tiles: each petal about 15 mm long and 10 mm wide at the base. Tapering to a rounded tip. You will need about 30 to 35 tiles.

Lay the paper cone former on the work surface. Beginning at the base of the cone. Press tiles onto the cone in a row, overlapping each tile by about one-third over the tile below. The tile tips point toward the cone tip.

Continue adding rows. Offset by half a tile width each row to stagger the joins as real roof tiles are laid. Press each tile firmly so it adheres to the underlying clay without gaps. The final row at the tip overlaps to meet at the cone apex.
Slide the paper cone out gently. The tiled roof cone holds its shape. Place on a ceramic tile and cure at 110 to 130 degrees C for 25 minutes. Allow to cool.

The roof rests on top of the cured wall shell. It is designed to be removable rather than permanently fixed, so the house interior is accessible. If a fixed roof is preferred, attach with jewellery-grade cyanoacrylate adhesive.
Stage 3: decorative details

Roll thin snakes of green clay about 1 mm in diameter. Arrange them on the wall surfaces in vine patterns. Curving from the base upward toward the windows and door frame. Press onto the cured wall surface firmly.

Roll tiny balls of coloured clay, 2 to 3 mm in diameter, for flowers. Press each ball lightly onto the vine at intervals to suggest blooms. Use the needle tool to indent a five-petal pattern into each ball if desired.

Add a door panel in a contrasting colour. Cut a small arched rectangle from clay and texture it with horizontal grain lines using the needle tool. Press it onto the door opening. If the door opening was cut through the wall. Press the door panel flat against the interior wall face. The arch will be visible from outside.
Place the fully decorated house shell on a ceramic tile and
cure
for a final 30 minutes at 110 to 130 degrees C to fix all the decorative additions. Allow to cool.
Curing

This project uses three separate curing sessions: walls alone, roof alone, and final detail pass. Do not attempt to cure all elements together in one session as the uncured tile and vine clay would deform under its own weight before hardening. Allow each cured component to cool completely before adding raw clay to it.
Finishing

No sanding is required for a textured piece like this. Apply a coat of satin varnish to the walls and a coat of gloss varnish to the roof tiles to differentiate the surface qualities. The windows can be filled after curing with a small amount of clear jewellery resin if a glazed effect is wanted.
Care

This piece is not food-safe and is for display only. Do not place in a microwave or dishwasher. Dust with a soft brush or cloth. The house is suitable for indoor display or a sheltered outdoor fairy garden position. Avoid prolonged exposure to rain or direct sun.
Variations
Tudor framing: press thin black clay strips onto cream walls in a half-timbered pattern before the final cure.
Hobbit hole: build a rounded dome rather than a box by pressing clay over a crumpled foil ball former. Removing the foil before curing.
Toadstool roof: substitute a hemispherical dome roof in red with white spots for a mushroom-house effect.
Larger scale: scale the wall former up to 80 mm per side for a more substantial house suitable as a centrepiece in a fairy garden planter.

rows.fix: Brace the interior with a crumpled ball of baking parchment until in the oven. Ensure wall slabs are 3 mm minimum and corner joins are smoothed from the inside
rows.cause: Walls too thin (less than 3 mm) or corners not properly joined
rows.problem: Walls collapsing inward when the cardboard former is removed

rows.fix: Use a cone made from card rather than thin paper, which is more heat-stable. Press tiles firmly and overlap generously
rows.cause: Tiles not pressed firmly enough, or the paper cone shrinking in the oven
rows.problem: Roof tiles lifting off the cone during curing

rows.fix: Press very firmly and add a tiny amount of liquid polymer clay as an adhesive between the raw vine and the cured wall, then cure immediately
rows.cause: Cured polymer clay does not bond raw clay as firmly as raw-to-raw joins
rows.problem: Vine snakes not staying attached to the cured wall surface when pressed on

rows.fix: Tent the whole house loosely with a foil dome during the final cure to moderate direct radiant heat on thin details
rows.cause: Thin elements heat faster and may exceed safe temperature before the mass of the piece reaches curing temperature
rows.problem: Scorching or browning on thin details (flowers, vine tips)

initialFindings.message: tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
paragraph reads at grade 11.8 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
initialFindings.snippet: Larger scale: scale the wall former up to 80 mm per side for a more substantial house suitable as a centrepiece in a fairy garden planter.
